Supply-Side Medicine
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Imagine that while sitting down to dinner one night your spouse suddenly collapses and is rushed to the hospital in cardiac arrest. In the intensive care unit, the doctor says that a balloon angioplasty is needed as soon as possible.  But in order to have the procedure, your loved one will have to be transported overseas -- if the angioplasty is even available. 

     Such a scenario could take place only in some undeveloped country, right? Actually, this could soon be the case in the United States, which faces a potentially severe shortage of raw medical materials.

Boyhood Memories of Quilting Time in Alabama
nytimes_articles.abstract:
EACH fall, after harvest, my grandmother Faith Smith and several of her friends turned to patching quilts and gossiping in her living room in New Brockton, Ala. Outside on the porch the men shelled peanuts. 

     It was a time of furious activity. The men pushed the furniture to the fringes of my grandmother's living room, and in the center they set up a makeshift quilting frame, made of two wooden sawhorses connected by two straight boards. All summer long, the women had collected torn and tattered clothes, sheets and bedspreads for this moment.

wiki_events.headline: Comet Hyakutake is imaged by the USA Asteroid Orbiter Near Earth Asteroid Rendezvous.

pages.title: Comet Hyakutake
pages.abstract: Comet Hyakutake is a comet discovered on 31 January 1996. It was dubbed the Great Comet of 1996; its passage to within 0.1 AU (15 Gm) of the Earth on 25 March was one of the closest cometary approaches of the previous 200 years. Reaching an apparent visual magnitude of zero and spanning nearly 80°, Hyakutake appeared very bright in the night sky and was widely seen around the world. The comet temporarily upstaged the much anticipated Comet Hale–Bopp, which was approaching the inner Solar System at the time.

pages.title: NEAR Shoemaker
pages.abstract: Near Earth Asteroid Rendezvous – Shoemaker, renamed after its 1996 launch in honor of planetary scientist Eugene Shoemaker, was a robotic space probe designed by the Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory for NASA to study the near-Earth asteroid Eros from close orbit over a period of a year. It was the first spacecraft to orbit an asteroid and land on it successfully. In February 2000, the mission closed in on the asteroid and orbited it. On February 12, 2001, Shoemaker touched down on the asteroid and was terminated just over two weeks later.

A Legal Star Is Sentenced For Robbing A Dallas Bank
nytimes_articles.abstract:
At Wednesday's sentencing of a 30-year-old lawyer who had robbed a bank of $1,430, even the judge was puzzled and saddened. 

     "This is probably one of the most unfortunate cases I've run across in my career, both as a lawyer and a judge," Judge Jorge A. Solis of Federal District Court said, before telling Joseph E. Chavis Jr. to report to Federal prison on May 1 to serve a 30-month sentence. "I guess sometimes, there's just no explanation for things."

The Nuclear Stockpile America Needs
nytimes_articles.abstract:
April 26 will mark the 10th anniversary of the explosion at the Chernobyl nuclear plant near Kiev, Ukraine, which sent a radioactive cloud into the atmosphere of Eastern Europe. 

     Today, an epidemic of childhood thyroid cancer marks the area most affected by the fallout -- Ukraine, western Russia and Belarus. In Belarus alone, the rate for this disease climbed from 1 per million in the decade before the Chernobyl accident to 36 cases per million in 1995, according to the World Health Organization.
